description: Navya Partner Sync — symptom + mood combination recommendations

recommendations.partner_note: She's in real physical pain today — her breasts are engorged and she's having a rough time.
partner_actions: Bring her a warm compress or warm towel before each feed — warmth helps relieve engorgement
Make sure she has water next to her every time she sits to feed
Take the baby for 30 mins after a feed so she can rest without holding anyone
Don't ask her to do anything else today — just be present
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't suggest she 'push through' or comment on her feeding frequency — it adds pressure she doesn't need.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Her body is doing something incredible. The pain is real and it won't last forever.

recommendations.partner_note: She's emotionally overwhelmed today and feeling really low. She needs your presence more than solutions.
partner_actions: Sit with her — don't try to fix it, just be there
Say 'I see how hard you're working. You're doing an amazing job' — and mean it
Take night duty tonight so she gets one longer sleep stretch
Order her favourite food — no cooking today
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't say 'you should be happy, the baby is healthy' — it dismisses what she's feeling.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Baby blues peak around Days 3–5. What she's feeling is real, valid, and temporary.

recommendations.partner_note: She's having a really hard day — both physical pain and emotional overwhelm at the same time. She needs maximum support right now.
partner_actions: Clear your schedule as much as possible today
Warm compress ready before every feed — don't wait for her to ask
Hold her hand or sit close — physical presence matters hugely right now
Handle all visitors, calls, and household decisions today — she shouldn't have to think
Tonight: you take the baby, she sleeps
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't leave her alone for long stretches. Don't minimise either the physical pain or the emotional state.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Two hard things at once is genuinely exhausting. She is not weak — she is doing the hardest thing.

recommendations.partner_note: She's anxious about whether she's producing enough milk — this is one of the most common postpartum fears and it's consuming her today.
partner_actions: Reassure her: 'Supply follows demand — you're doing everything right'
Make sure she's eating enough — new moms need 500 extra calories while breastfeeding
Prepare a lactation-friendly snack: methi ladoo, warm milk with turmeric, or dal
Offer to research a lactation consultant together — not as a fix, as support
Keep her water bottle filled all day
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't suggest formula as a solution unless she brings it up — it may feel like you've lost faith in her.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Worrying about supply is almost universal. Feed frequently and stay hydrated — her body is responding.

recommendations.partner_note: ⚠️ She may have mastitis — a breast infection. This needs medical attention today, not tomorrow.
partner_actions: Call her doctor or gynaecologist right now — this is urgent
Don't wait to 'see if it gets better' — mastitis needs prompt treatment
Arrange the appointment and go with her if possible
In the meantime: keep her hydrated, help her rest, and take the baby
She can usually continue feeding — but her doctor will confirm
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't delay. Don't suggest home remedies as a substitute for medical care today.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: This is not her fault. Mastitis is common and very treatable — getting help quickly is the right move.

recommendations.partner_note: The baby is cluster feeding — feeding constantly for hours. She's exhausted and likely doubting herself. This is completely normal but brutal.
partner_actions: Tell her: 'Cluster feeding means your supply is building — you're doing it right'
Set up a comfortable feeding station — pillow, water, snacks, phone charger
Take over everything else: food, dishes, door, other kids
When the baby finally sleeps — she sleeps first, chores can wait
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't ask 'is the baby getting enough?' — it adds to her anxiety. Trust the process with her.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Cluster feeding is temporary and a sign her body is working. It usually peaks and passes within 24–48 hours.

recommendations.partner_note: She's feeding through real pain today — cracked nipples are genuinely agonising and she's tired on top of it.
partner_actions: Get nipple cream if you don't have it — ask the pharmacist for lanolin cream
Help her check the baby's latch using a YouTube tutorial together — a better latch stops the pain
After each feed, let her rest for 10 mins before taking the baby
Warm compress before, cool compress after each feed
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't suggest she 'take a break from feeding' without medical guidance — latch improvement is the real fix.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Feeding through this level of discomfort takes incredible dedication. If pain continues past Day 3, a latch check can fix it fast.

recommendations.partner_note: She's tired and emotional today. She may not even know why she's crying — and that's okay. She just needs to feel not alone.
partner_actions: Put your arm around her without saying anything first
Ask: 'What would feel good right now — do you want to talk or just sit together?'
Handle the night shift tonight — let her sleep 4 consecutive hours minimum
Send a voice note during the day just saying you're thinking of her
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't problem-solve immediately. Don't say 'just sleep when the baby sleeps' — it's not that simple.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Being tired and emotional at the same time is not weakness — it's what happens when a person gives everything they have.

recommendations.partner_note: She has a tender lump in her breast — likely a blocked duct. It needs attention now before it becomes an infection.
partner_actions: Help her apply a warm compress to the lump before feeding
Gently remind her to feed from that side first while it's most full
Look up gentle massage technique for blocked ducts and offer to help
Watch for fever — if she develops one, call the doctor immediately
Keep her hydrated and rested as much as possible
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't ignore it hoping it resolves — blocked ducts can become mastitis within 24 hours.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Catching this early means it can usually be cleared without medication. She's doing the right thing by flagging it.

recommendations.partner_note: The baby keeps falling asleep mid-feed — she's spending hours trying to complete a feed and it's wearing her out.
partner_actions: Sit with her during feeds so she's not alone in the frustration
Learn the 'tickle feet' trick together — gently tickle baby's feet to keep them awake
Try skin-to-skin — undress the baby down to nappy during feeds
After a feed attempt, take the baby so she can close her eyes even for 20 mins
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't express frustration about feeding taking long — she's already frustrated enough for both of you.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Sleepy feeders are common in newborns, especially in the first 2 weeks. It gets better as the baby gets stronger.

recommendations.partner_note: She's managing okay today but dealing with engorgement — she could use a little extra care without being fussed over.
partner_actions: Quietly make sure there's a warm compress ready for her
Keep her water bottle topped up throughout the day
Offer to take the baby after feeds so she can decompress
Ask once: 'Is there anything that would make today easier?' — then actually do it
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't hover — she's okay and just needs small practical support, not constant check-ins.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Engorgement usually peaks Day 3–5 and eases as feeding regulates. She's through the hardest part.

recommendations.partner_note: She's doing okay today but has an undercurrent of anxiety about milk supply. A little reassurance from you goes a long way.
partner_actions: Make her a warm meal — dal, methi sabzi, or anything with fenugreek
Tell her you've noticed how hard she's working and you're proud of her
Ensure she drinks at least 2.5–3L of water today — offer it proactively
If she wants to talk about it, listen without jumping to solutions
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't say 'you're probably overthinking it' — her anxiety is real even if the supply is fine.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Milk supply anxiety is almost universal in new moms. Frequent feeding and good hydration are the two most evidence-backed solutions.

recommendations.partner_note: She's handling cluster feeding okay today — just make sure she doesn't run on empty.
partner_actions: Keep snacks and water within arm's reach of her feeding spot
Handle dinner tonight — don't ask what she wants, just make something nourishing
Give her a genuine compliment on how she's handling it
Offer a 1-hour baby handover so she can shower and breathe
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't make cluster feeding a bigger deal than she is — match her energy, don't escalate.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Getting through cluster feeding days with this kind of resilience is genuinely impressive.

recommendations.partner_note: She's in good spirits today despite some physical discomfort — support her without making it a big deal.
partner_actions: Pick up nipple cream on your way home if she doesn't have it
Tell her she looks like she's finding her rhythm — because she is
Offer a short walk outside together — fresh air on a good day is powerful
Let her choose how to spend one hour today, completely her way
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't focus on the physical symptom today — she's doing well emotionally, protect that energy.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: A good day is a good day. The physical healing is happening even when it doesn't feel like it.

recommendations.partner_note: She's doing well today — just dealing with a sleepy feeder. Keep the good energy going.
partner_actions: Celebrate her good day — say it out loud
Learn the tickle-feet trick together and make it a team thing
Suggest a short outing or something she enjoys — she's earned it
Take a photo of her with the baby today — she'll want this memory
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Nothing major — just don't let a good day go unacknowledged.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Good days are momentum. She's building something real.

recommendations.partner_note: She's having a genuinely good day with no symptoms flagged. This is the moment to connect, not just support.
partner_actions: Tell her you've noticed she's doing well and you're proud of her
Plan something small she'd enjoy — even just her favourite dessert
Ask about something non-baby: her interests, her friends, her feelings about the future
Take a proper family photo today — document the good days
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't treat a good day as an opportunity to catch up on your own to-do list and disappear.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: She deserves to enjoy the good days fully. Help her do that.

recommendations.partner_note: She's feeling great today — celebrate this together. These moments matter.
partner_actions: Make a big deal of it — she worked hard to get to a great day
Do something together as a family, even just a short walk
Ask her what made today feel good — and remember it for harder days
Give her uninterrupted time to call a friend or do something just for herself
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't use her great day to bring up logistics, finances, or chores — protect the energy.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: She's getting there. Great days become more frequent. This is what recovery looks like.

recommendations.partner_note: She's in great spirits even through a tough cluster feeding stretch — she's found her footing. Celebrate that.
partner_actions: Tell her you're in awe of how she's handling everything
Set up her feeding spot extra nicely today — her favourite show, snacks, comfort
Take a video of her with the baby — she's in a good place and won't always remember it
Plan something for her to look forward to this week
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Nothing to fix today — just amplify the good.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Handling hard things with a smile is a superpower. She has it.

recommendations.partner_note: She's in pain and she's emotional — a really hard combination. She needs you to step up fully today.
partner_actions: Drop everything non-essential today
Get lanolin nipple cream immediately if you don't have it
Sit with her during feeds — don't leave her alone with the pain
Cook or order her absolute favourite food
Tonight: full night duty, no exceptions
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't try to silver-line it. Don't say 'it'll get better' without first saying 'I see how hard this is right now.'
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Two hard things at once takes twice the strength. She has it — and she doesn't have to do it alone today.

recommendations.partner_note: She's exhausted, engorged, and worried about supply — a physically and mentally draining combination. Maximum practical support today.
partner_actions: Warm compress before every single feed — make it your job today
Prepare lactation-supporting meals: methi dal, warm milk, oats
Reassure her with facts: 'Engorgement actually means your supply is strong'
Handle every non-feeding responsibility so she can focus on rest and feeding
Don't mention formula unless she does — it will feel like giving up to her
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't let her go more than 2 hours without water or food today.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Engorgement and supply anxiety often peak at the same time — and then resolve together. She's at the hardest point.

recommendations.partner_note: She's okay but has a blocked duct that needs attention before it gets worse. Help her stay on top of it today.
partner_actions: Warm compress on the lump before each feed — set a reminder if needed
Look up the gentle massage technique for blocked ducts together
Monitor for fever — if she develops one, call the doctor same day
Keep her hydrated and make sure she's not skipping meals
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't wait and see with a blocked duct — early action prevents mastitis.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: She caught it early. With warm compress and frequent feeding, most blocked ducts clear within 24–48 hours.

recommendations.partner_note: ⚠️ URGENT — possible mastitis AND emotional crisis. She needs medical help and emotional support simultaneously. This is a critical day.
partner_actions: Call the doctor RIGHT NOW — don't delay
Stay with her — don't leave her alone today
Hold her hand, be physically present, say 'we're handling this together'
Take full charge of the baby so she can focus on getting well
After the doctor: favourite food, rest, no responsibilities
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Do not minimise either issue. Do not leave her to manage the doctor's appointment alone.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Getting help today is the strongest thing she can do. She is not failing — she is fighting for herself and her baby.

recommendations.partner_note: No specific symptoms today — just tired. The invisible exhaustion of new motherhood is real even without a flagged symptom.
partner_actions: Let her sleep in tomorrow — take the first morning feed
Don't wait for her to ask for help — anticipate and act
Make dinner, tidy up, and let her sit down without a task list
A simple 'you're doing an amazing job' before bed goes a long way
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't compare her tiredness to yours. Postpartum exhaustion is physiologically different.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Rest is recovery. She's allowed to be tired — and she deserves support even on the ordinary days.

recommendations.partner_note: She's having a rough day with no specific physical symptom — this is often harder to explain, which makes it lonelier. Just be there.
partner_actions: Ask 'How are YOU — not the baby, you' and actually wait for the answer
Don't try to diagnose or fix it — just listen
Take the baby for at least 2 hours today so she has space
If she wants to talk, listen. If she wants silence, sit with her in it
Check in again in the evening — one check-in isn't enough on a rough day
recommendations.what_not_to_do: Don't say 'but everything is fine' — that's not what she needs to hear right now.
recommendations.affirmation_for_her: Some days are just hard without a reason. That is allowed. She doesn't have to explain or justify it.

fallback_recommendation.partner_note: She checked in today — that means she's taking care of herself, which takes courage.
partner_actions: Ask her how she's really doing today
Make sure she's eaten a proper meal
Take the baby for at least 30 minutes so she can breathe
Tell her you see how hard she's working
fallback_recommendation.what_not_to_do: Don't assume she's fine just because she hasn't said otherwise.
fallback_recommendation.affirmation_for_her: Showing up every day is enough. She is enough.
